channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: A $2.5 Billion Whodunit: The Hack That Dented the U.K. Economy
item.description: A loose collective of cybercriminals initially took credit for crippling Jaguar Land Rover last year. Investigators now see Russian hands behind the ransomware attack.
item.dc:creator: Adam Goldman, Jane Bradley, Dustin Volz and Michael Schwirtz
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Land Rovers being transported from the Jaguar Land Rover factory in Solihull, England, last year.

item.title: World Cups Fans in Myanmar Boycott Junta-Linked Broadcasts
item.description: In Myanmar, games are being broadcast by a company that is co-owned by the military, which usurped power five years ago. Some fans are trying other ways to watch.
item.dc:creator: Richard C. Paddock
item.media:credit: Catherine Ivill — AMA/Getty Images
item.media:description: An informal boycott of official World Cup broadcasts has spread across Myanmar.

item.title: Deadly Heat Wave in Europe Continues to Smash Temperature Records
item.description: The continent’s second heat wave since May continued to disrupt daily life for millions. Officials in Spain said it might have contributed to a spike in deaths.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel, Nazaneen Ghaffar, Aurelien Breeden and Zane Irwin
item.media:credit: Oscar Del Pozo/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Construction workers in Madrid this week.

item.title: Can a Youth Boom Survive South Sudan’s Education Crisis?
item.description: South Sudan is the world’s newest nation and its education system is on the verge of collapse, putting an entire generation at risk.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg and Ed Ram
item.category: United States Agency for International Development
item.media:credit: Ed Ram for The New York Times

item.title: Iraq’s New Leader Faces Resistance Trying to Bring Militias Under State Control
item.description: The Trump administration has demanded that Iraqi leaders distance themselves from Iran and rein in Iran-linked militias that are outside of government control.
item.dc:creator: Raja Abdulrahim and Falih Hassan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Ahmad Al-Rubaye/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Members of the Peace Brigades militia during a ceremony in the city of Samarra, Iraq, in June, marking their separation from the Sadrist movement and their integration into the Iraqi security forces.

item.title: Iran Strikes Ship in Strait of Hormuz, Undermining Efforts to Restore Traffic
item.description: The attack, confirmed by U.S. and Iranian officials, came after Tehran threatened to disrupt shipping in the waterway and as Washington sought regional support for its peace agreement.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis, Farnaz Fassihi, Jenny Gross and Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Elke Scholiers/Getty Images
item.media:description: Oil tankers and cargo vessels off the coast of Oman near the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday.

item.title: To Escape the Heat, Parisians Jump into an Old Industrial Canal
item.description: Crowds are filling the Canal Saint-Martin in Paris, as temperatures soar above 100 degrees Fahrenheit. Forty people drowned while swimming in other waterways.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler and Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:credit: Samuel Boivin/NurPhoto, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Swimming in Canal St. Martin in Paris on Tuesday.

item.title: King Charles III Has Paid More Than $39.6 Million in Taxes, Palace Discloses in a U.K. First
item.description: Charles released his tax information for the period he has been king of Britain as part of a push for greater transparency after the scandal involving the former Prince Andrew.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Alastair Grant/Associated Press
item.media:description: King Charles III and Queen Camilla at the Royal Ascot horse races last week.

item.title: A Right-Wing Election Victory in Colombia, With a Little Help from Trump
item.description: The U.S. president endorsed the right-wing winner. But his influence went further.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal and Julie Turkewitz
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Supporters of Abelardo de la Espriella in Barranquilla, Colombia, on Sunday during the presidential runoff election.

item.title: Officials Shut Down Kenya’s Capital to Block Protest
item.description: The protest, an annual event, has become a showcase of the growing political power of Kenya’s youth.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg and Brian O. Otieno
item.media:credit: Luis Tato/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Protesters being arrested outside Parliament buildings in Nairobi on Thursday.

item.title: A Puppy-Abuse Case in China, Caught on Video, Prompts Rare Public Protest
item.description: After a man was filmed torturing dogs, hundreds gathered in a rare demonstration that the police tried to break up. For many, the sit-in was their first civic action.
item.dc:creator: Murphy Zhao and Lily Kuo
item.media:credit: Video obtained by The New York Times

item.title: North Korea Deploys Its First Destroyer, Vowing to Project Nuclear Power by Sea
item.description: The 5,000-ton Choe Hyon is the largest warship North Korea has ever built and is said to be armed with nuclear-capable missiles.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:credit: Korean Central News Agency, via Reuters
item.media:description: A photo released by North Korean state media is said to show its leader, Kim Jong-un, center, at the commissioning ceremony of a new destroyer, the Choe Hyon, at Nampo port on Tuesday.

item.title: Iran’s Loyalists Promote a Wider Nationalism, Unveiled Women Included
item.description: Government supporters are showing off new ties with alleged former dissidents in a bid to show that they can withstand enemies at home as well as abroad.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon and Sanam Mahoozi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A pro-government demonstration in Tehran last month where some of the women were partially veiled and some dressed more conservatively.

item.title: Myanmar’s Civil War Has Become an Apocalypse
item.description: Overshadowed by conflicts in Ukraine, Gaza and Lebanon, the fighting in Myanmar’s isolated heartland has reached new depths.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Beech and Daniel Berehulak
item.category: Coups D'Etat and Attempted Coups D'Etat
War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Daniel Berehulak/The New York Times
item.media:description: On a forgotten frontline, the rebels fight deep in the jungle.

item.title: In London, You Can Celebrate America’s 250th Birthday From the British Side
item.description: Britain may have lost the Revolutionary War, but London still holds the blueprints of the American Revolution. Follow in the founders’ footsteps at these notable places.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses and Jeremie Souteyrat
item.media:description: Inside Middle Temple Hall, a 16th-century, wood-paneled hall where legal scholars have long gathered.

item.title: The Aftermath of Venezuela’s Earthquakes
item.description: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez, a photographer for The New York Times, describes the scene on the ground after the Venezuelan earthquakes.
item.dc:creator: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez, Leila Medina and Estelle Caswell

item.title: Congo Ebola Crisis: Contact Tracing Is Dangerously Behind, Officials Warn
item.description: Most of the people testing positive for Ebola in the Democratic Republic of Congo are not on health workers’ radar, suggesting that contact tracing is lagging dangerously behind.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg and Stephanie Nolen
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A community health worker taking the temperature of a man whose son is being treated for the virus near the city of Bunia in Democratic Republic of Congo.

item.title: How Long Can a Person Survive Under Rubble?
item.description: It depends on water, air and how quickly rescuers are able to find people.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rescue teams walking through a building that collapsed during the earthquakes in Venezuela.

item.title: Hoping for Miracles as Venezuela Digs Out From Massive Quakes
item.description: It is known as a “doublet,” two earthquakes in quick succession, and it has brought the Latin American country to its knees.
item.dc:creator: María Victoria Fermín, Anatoly Kurmanaev, Julie Turkewitz, Isayen Herrera and Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Looking for survivors on Thursday in the rubble of a building in Catia La Mar, Venezuela, that collapsed a day earlier in an earthquake.

item.title: Less Islamism, More Nationalism
item.description: In Iran and across the Middle East, the bond between religion and politics is getting weaker.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: On the streets of Tehran.

item.title: Aid official says groups are mobilizing to provide shelter and water to quake victims.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: First responders look over a partially collapsed building in Catia La Mar, Venezuela.

item.title: Tighter Canadian Borders Limit U.S. Deportees Seeking Refuge
item.description: New Canadian policies and a longstanding border agreement with the United States will make heading north a tough route for Syrians and Haitians.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:credit: Nathan Denette/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Trudeau welcoming Syrian refugees to Toronto in December 2015.

item.title: After Supreme Court TPS Ruling, What Conditions May Haitian and Syrian Deportees Face?
item.description: The Supreme Court ruled that the Trump administration could end humanitarian protections for many Haitians and Syrians in the United States, clearing the way for their potential deportation.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Haitian physician who fled his homeland in 2021, at his home in Springfield, Ohio, in January. About 350,000 Haitians had received protection from deportation in the United States.

item.title: Global Warming Upends London Climate Week
item.description: The extreme heat smothering Europe has upended Climate Week in London.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Yui Mok
item.media:description: King Charles III at a London Climate Week reception on Wednesday at St. James’s Palace.

item.title: Devastating Earthquakes Will Test Venezuela’s Newfound Alliance With U.S.
item.description: President Trump has boasted of the success of an armed intervention in Venezuela. A natural disaster raises pressure to match rhetoric with action.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A person searches the ruins of an apartment building in Catia La Mar, Venezuela, on Thursday.

item.title: On the ground and online, Venezuelans desperately search for missing relatives.
item.description: Friends and family of those in the earthquake zone were turning to social media and heading to affected neighborhoods as they awaited an official rescue response.
item.dc:creator: María Victoria Fermín, Sheyla Urdaneta and Frances Robles
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Searching for survivors in the rubble in Catia La Mar, Venezuela, on Thursday.

item.title: After Venezuela Earthquakes, La Guaira Residents Search for Loved Ones in the Rubble
item.description: A missing child. A boyfriend in the rubble. Venezuelans on Thursday were still trying to understand the scope of the devastation in this hard-hit city.
item.dc:creator: Isayen Herrera, Julie Turkewitz and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:description: In Catia La Mar, a port city in La Guaira State in Venezuela, residents said they had seen few rescue workers at the scene after the earthquakes on Wednesday night, leaving civilians to look for survivors.

item.title: How to help victims of the Venezuela earthquakes.
item.description: Here’s how to contribute to organizations that are aiding the rescue and recovery efforts in Venezuela.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rescue efforts in Caracas, Venezuela, on Wednesday after two large earthquakes struck the country.

item.title: Latest Victims of France’s Heat Wave: Children Locked in Family Cars
item.description: As temperatures pass record levels in France, the heat wave-related death toll continues to rise.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler and Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:credit: Joann Pai for The New York Times
item.media:description: Along the seine in Paris on Wednesday.

item.title: Why the Venezuelan Earthquakes Happened, and What to Expect Next
item.description: Twin earthquakes like those that ripped through the region are unusual but not unheard of. Scientists are already gathering data needed for a more detailed picture.
item.dc:creator: Robin George Andrews
item.media:credit: Leonardo Fernandez Viloria/Reuters
item.media:description: Emergency workers at the site of a collapsed building in Caracas on Thursday.

item.title: Tickled Apes Reveal the Rhythmic Roots of Laughter
item.description: A study of chimps, gorillas and other great apes, including human children, sheds light on how laughter has evolved.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes
item.media:credit: Emil Lippe for The New York Times
item.media:description: All the planet’s great apes laugh, and they often do so in the same kind of regular, repeating rhythm that humans do, according to a new study.

item.title: Supreme Court Expands Trump’s Power Over Immigration With TPS and Southern Border Decisions
item.description: The court handed President Trump victories in his push to rescind deportation protections for hundreds of thousands of people and turn away migrants at the southern border.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court’s 6-to-3 decision in two cases with broad implications divided along ideological lines, with the liberal justices dissenting.

item.title: Driver Plows Into Crowd of World Cup Fans in Mexico, Authorities Say
item.description: Videos show a car repeatedly accelerating into a crowd in Cabo San Lucas after Mexico won a World Cup game. The authorities said at least 17 people were injured and the driver was arrested.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe and James McManagan
item.media:credit: Alfredo Estrella/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A file photo shows tourists in Los Cabos, an area of Baja California Sur, Mexico, which includes the city of Cabo San Lucas.

item.title: Earthquakes Are Latest Challenge for Venezuelan Leader
item.description: President Delcy Rodríguez, in power only for a few months, has been trying to balance the demands of President Trump and her own domestic political needs.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.media:credit: Federico Parra/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: President Delcy Rodríguez became leader after the U.S. military deposed and captured her predecessor, Nicolás Maduro.

item.title: Venezuela Earthquakes Hammer Infrastructure, Making Aid Efforts Harder
item.description: The power grid, water supply and health system were already under strain from years of neglect and underinvestment.
item.dc:creator: María Victoria Fermín and John Yoon
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rubble of a residential building that collapsed during an earthquake in Caracas, on Wednesday.

item.title: Two Earthquakes Hit Venezuela: What to Know About Death Toll, Damage and Rescue Response
item.description: Two major quakes struck back to back on Wednesday night in the country’s populated northern areas. The second was the strongest to hit Venezuela in over a century.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Emergency workers tried to rescue people trapped in the rubble of a residential building in Caracas, Venezuela, on Wednesday.

item.title: La Guaira, Venezuelan Port Town, Is Hit Hard by Earthquakes
item.description: Deadly mudslides in 1999 dealt lasting blows to impoverished La Guaira. The response of the acting president, Delcy Rodríguez, may be a bellwether of her political longevity.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev

item.title: Several Strong Quakes Hit Across the World in 24 Hours
item.description: Experts said the powerful earthquakes that struck California and Japan are likely not connected to the devastating tremors in Venezuela.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff and Francesca Regalado
item.media:credit: Kyodo, via Reuters
item.media:description: A pedestrian passes a damaged wall of a building following an earthquake in Hachinohe, Aomori Prefecture, Japan, on Thursday.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: María Victoria Fermín, Anatoly Kurmanaev, Julie Turkewitz and Isayen Herrera

item.title: Turkey or Türkiye? Who Is the U.S. Playing?
item.description: The push to rebrand the country, which started in the last decade, was inspired by politicians and coincided with an international public relations campaign.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:credit: Richard Heathcote/Getty Images

item.title: Deadly Heat Scorches Europe
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel, Nazaneen Ghaffar, Aurelien Breeden and Zane Irwin

item.title: Venezuela Residents Describe Terror as Deadly Earthquake Struck
item.description: Buildings collapsed, windows rattled and residents poured into the streets in Venezuela’s capital and nearby cities after two major earthquakes hit.
item.dc:creator: Genevieve Glatsky, Tibisay Romero, Fabiola Ferrero, Julie Turkewitz, María Victoria Fermín and Yan Zhuang
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A couple cries in front of a building that collapsed during the earthquakes that struck Venezuela on Wednesday.

item.title: Venezuela’s Neighbors Offer Aid in Earthquake Response
item.description: Officials from the United States, El Salvador and the Dominican Republic said they would send rescue teams and humanitarian assistance to Caracas.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Emergency services searching the rubble of a collapsed building on Wednesday.

item.title: 7.5-Magnitude Quake Is Largest to Hit Venezuela in More Than a Century
item.description: The U.S. Geological Survey estimated that it could be far deadlier than a 7.7-magnitude earthquake that struck the country in 1900.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Photos: Venezuela Earthquakes Cause Death and Destruction in Caracas, La Guaira and Beyond
item.description: Two powerful quakes shook the South American country, causing death and destruction.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Civilians and emergency workers walking through the rubble of a building that collapsed during an earthquake, on Thursday in Catia La Mar, Venezuela.

item.title: Venezuela Hit by 7.1-Magnitude Earthquake
item.dc:creator: María Victoria Fermín and Simon Romero

item.title: A Climate Chaos Maker
item.description: Global warming is causing extreme heat around the world. Now El Niño, a natural phenomenon that disrupts global weather patterns, is also at work.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:credit: Joann Pai for The New York Times
item.media:description: Outside the Louvre museum in Paris, yesterday.

item.title: Cuba Announces Sweeping Overhaul of Struggling Communist Economy, But Obstacles Remain
item.description: The Cuban government announced a major economic restructuring, but experts say most changes would depend on buy-in from the Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles and David C. Adams
item.media:credit: Eliana Aponte for The New York Times
item.media:description: Workers in the production workshop of Dforja Creations, a private furniture company in Havana, in 2024.

item.title: ICC Judges Sue Trump Administration Over Sanctions
item.description: Kimberly Prost, a Canadian judge at the International Criminal Court, and two colleagues are suing the administration, saying the sanctions were a form of retaliation.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek and Amanda Taub
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Geert Vanden Wijngaert/Reuters
item.media:description: Kimberly Prost, a Canadian judge at the International Criminal Court, along with two others, is suing President Trump and his administration, claiming the penalties exceeded his authority.

item.title: Why Is Europe the Fastest-Warming Continent
item.description: The burning of fossil fuels is raising temperatures worldwide, but local factors, on land and at sea, determine which regions warm most rapidly.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:credit: Toby Shepheard/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Morning commuters on the London Bridge on Wednesday, where temperatures reached 96 degrees Fahrenheit, the hottest recorded June day in the United Kingdom.

item.title: See Record-Breaking Extreme Heat Wave Across Europe in Photos
item.description: Temperature records have been broken in several European countries this week.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: A woman cooling herself with a hand-held fan during the heat wave in Paris, on Thursday.

item.title: Bahrain Restricts Shiite Holy Day Amid Iran War Crackdown
item.description: The restrictions are the latest in a string of measures Bahrain has taken against Shiite citizens in the Gulf country, with many accused of having loyalties to Iran.
item.dc:creator: Ismaeel Naar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Mohammed Al-Shaikh/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Observing Ashura in the village of Sanabis, west of Manama, Bahrain, in 2015.

item.title: The Wife of Spain’s Leader Is Accused of Graft. It May Actually Help Him.
item.description: People close to Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez keep being investigated for corruption. Analysts say weaknesses in the latest case could help him discredit the others.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz
item.media:credit: Bernat Armangue/Associated Press
item.media:description: Spain’s prime minister, Pedro Sánchez, and his wife, Begoña Gómez, in Barcelona this month.
